positions.position: If it were up to me, we would all start building tools that use modified markdown and pass markdown files left and right.
Markdown is not a suitable alternative to DOCX. When your counterparty sends you a redline in DOCX, your document processor and comparison software needs to handle that.
The real question is whether a standardised LLM interface for reading and modifying DOCX could eliminate enough friction that the format debate becomes moot.

sections.title: The Core Problem
sections.content:
DOCX is the dominant file format for legal document creation, negotiation, and execution worldwide. Lawyers spend the majority of their working day in Microsoft Word, producing and revising contracts, pleadings, correspondence, and other documents. The format has become so deeply embedded in legal workflows that any discussion of replacing it is immediately met with practical objections about backwards compatibility and the sheer scale of existing document repositories.

However, DOCX presents enormous challenges for AI-assisted workflows. The format's underlying XML is extraordinarily complex, with Word often breaking what appears to be continuous text into separate XML runs for no apparent formatting reason. Even between major implementations (Microsoft Word and Google Docs), the same DOCX file can render differently, causing formatting issues when documents move between systems. For AI agents that need to read, understand, and modify legal documents, this complexity creates a formidable barrier.

sections.title: Agent Skills and MCP Failures
sections.content:
Multiple community members have tested the available tools for AI-assisted DOCX editing and found them wanting. The Word MCP integration was described as not very intuitive, with functionality that is not as good as generating a script on the fly. One contributor noted that the MCP could be useful for search and replace operations but falls short for the complete rewrites and complex edits that legal work demands.

Existing agent skills for DOCX fail regularly. The DOCX agent skill available on GitHub was tested by several members who reported inconsistent results, particularly when writing to DOCX files. One contributor observed that generating a fresh Python script for each editing task actually produces better results than using the purpose-built skill, a damning indictment of the current tooling state.

The community has identified that Word's convoluted XML tagging is a root cause. Word breaks up words and sentences within separate runs even when there are no formatting differences, creating needless complexity that confuses AI models trying to make targeted edits.

sections.title: superdoc-redlines: A Purpose-Built Solution
sections.content:
The superdoc-redlines CLI tool emerged from the community as a purpose-built solution for one of the most critical DOCX workflows: document comparison and redlining. Rather than trying to make general-purpose AI tools work with the full complexity of DOCX, this tool focuses specifically on the comparison and markup workflow that is central to legal negotiation.

The tool was initially built as a pure CLI library, with discussion about whether to refactor it into an MCP server. The consensus leaned toward CLI-first design, with the observation that CLI tools plus skills work better than MCP because humans can use the CLI as well, and the downside of serializing to JSON is acceptable since agents handle piped data well. The library was recognised as impressively concise, with a core of under 200 lines of code -- the creator noted that figuring out what not to build was the hard part, having spent months going through over-engineered alternatives.

sections.title: The Footnotes and Eyebrows Problem
sections.content:
Beyond the basic challenge of reading and writing DOCX, legal documents contain structural elements that are particularly problematic for AI processing. Footnotes, endnotes, header/footer content (sometimes called "eyebrows"), margin notes, and tracked changes all carry legally significant information that naive document parsers may miss or misinterpret.

When a document is converted to markdown for AI processing, these structural elements lose their spatial context. A footnote that qualifies a critical clause may be separated from it by hundreds of lines in the flattened output. This context problem means that even when the text extraction is accurate, the AI may not properly associate qualifying language with the provisions it modifies.

sections.title: Towards Better DOCX Tooling
sections.content:
The community is converging on a pragmatic approach: rather than replacing DOCX, build better abstraction layers around it. The proposed standardised LLM interface would provide a consistent way for AI tools to read and modify DOCX files, shielding them from the underlying XML complexity.

A key architectural insight is the need to separate analysis from editing. The analysis phase (understanding what the document says, identifying issues, comparing against a playbook) can work on markdown or plain text extracted from the DOCX. The editing phase (actually modifying the document) must work with the DOCX format directly to preserve formatting, tracked changes, and other structural elements that lawyers depend on.

Cleaning up Word's XML as a preprocessing step was suggested as a practical intermediate solution -- normalising the XML to merge unnecessary run breaks and simplify the structure before AI interaction, then re-complexifying only as needed for Word compatibility.

references: Word MCP - Microsoft's Model Context Protocol server for Word
Open XML SDK - for programmatic DOCX manipulation
Jordan Bryan - "On the Immortality of Microsoft Word" (The Redline blog)
Jordan Bryan - "Why Won't Markdown Replace DOCX?" (LinkedIn)
openQuestions: Could a standardised LLM interface for DOCX editing be developed as an open-source project the community could rally around?
Is it possible to build a reliable XML cleaning layer that normalises Word's convoluted tagging without losing formatting fidelity?
How should footnotes and other spatial context be preserved when converting DOCX to LLM-friendly formats?

vibe-coding.title: Vibe Coding for Lawyers: Getting Started
vibe-coding.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 18 contributors

keyFindings.insight: The biggest barrier to lawyer-developers sharing their tools is not building them but making them installable by non-technical users. Open-sourcing on GitHub is insufficient when most lawyers do not have GitHub accounts or VS Code.

There is a fundamental tension between sandboxed platforms (Replit, Lovable, Google AI Studio) that offer frictionless experience but limited capability, and local environments (Cursor, Claude Code, Antigravity) that offer full power but require technical setup.

The "sledgehammer cracking a nut" approach -- brute-forcing solutions without deep understanding of the underlying technology -- is a valid and productive methodology for lawyer-coders, though it creates fragile code that needs hardening.
A series of progressive "how to get started" posts was identified as a high-value community need, walking lawyers from zero to their first deployed tool.

CLAUDE.md and equivalent project instruction files are critical for maintaining quality and consistency across coding sessions, though there is debate about whether to write them early or let them emerge from the project.

Security hardening of vibe-coded backends is a serious concern. Multiple contributors reported real security issues in AI-generated backend code, encouraging everyone not to have faith in vibe-coded backends.

practicalConsensus: Start with a sandboxed platform (Replit, Google AI Studio, Lovable) for your first project to minimize setup friction, then graduate to Cursor or Claude Code when you need more control.
Model your data structure before writing any code. Focus on what the data will look like and ensure that stays steady throughout development.
Use version control from day one. At minimum, when you have a working base in your main branch, create a branch to work on new features so you can revert if things go wrong.
Write CLAUDE.md or equivalent project instruction files to maintain consistency across sessions, but do not over-specify upfront -- let them evolve with the project.
Do not deploy vibe-coded applications with backends to production without security review. AI-generated code often contains real security vulnerabilities.
Consider Docker or portable executables for sharing tools with colleagues who cannot install development environments.
The BMAD method and test-driven development scaffolding are valuable for building anything with proper authentication, security, and API routes.

activeDebates.question: Sandboxed platforms vs local development environments for lawyer-coders?

positions.position: Asking lawyers to download Cursor or VS Code is too much effort. Suggest sandboxed platforms like Lovable, Manus, or Replit for maximum accessibility.
Sandboxed platforms hit a ceiling quickly. For anything beyond a simple prototype, you need the full IDE experience with proper file access, debugging, and terminal control.
Internal agent builders from YC startups are bridging this gap, allowing vibe-coded apps to make it to production with one click while remaining in a safe environment.

activeDebates.question: Is CLAUDE.md better written early or late in a project?

positions.position: Write it early and be prescriptive. The model adheres better to instructions when they are established from the start.
Let it emerge. Start with minimal instructions and add to them as you discover what the project needs. Over-specifying upfront leads to instructions the model cannot follow.
Use handoff files and constant GitHub pushes alongside CLAUDE.md. The context management problem is more important than when the file is created.

activeDebates.question: Should the community group be called "Advanced Lawyer Vibe-Coders" or something else?

positions.position: The "vibe coder" label is appropriate and helps normalise the practice among legal professionals who are not traditional developers.
The term invites criticism. Several community members have real development backgrounds; the "vibe" framing undersells the sophistication of what is being built.
The name "Legal Quants" better captures the idea of lawyers who quantify and automate their work, regardless of coding methodology.

sections.title: The Learning Curve
sections.content:
The community encompasses a wide spectrum of technical ability, from self-described vibe coders who brute-force solutions without deep technical understanding to certified ethical hackers with Python and C++ proficiency. This diversity creates a rich learning environment but also raises questions about how to onboard newcomers.

Several contributors described their approach candidly. One experienced litigator described themselves as a sledgehammer cracking a nut, admitting they do not really know what is going on but brute-force it until it works. Another contributor, an in-house counsel, noted that the goal matters: whether you want to sell a product (which vibe coders would likely fail at) or create a tool others can use, which is achievable.

The community identified a natural progression: start with AI-assisted prompt engineering in chat interfaces, graduate to sandboxed coding platforms, then move to full IDE environments. Each step unlocks more capability but requires more technical setup.

sections.title: Choosing Your Platform
sections.content:
The platform choice depends heavily on the user's technical comfort level and goals.

For beginners, Google AI Studio, Replit, and Lovable offer sandboxed environments where code runs in the cloud and deployment can be one-click. The major limitation is that these platforms cannot support complex backends, local file access, or advanced features like document processing pipelines.

For intermediate users, Cursor with Claude or Gemini models provides a VS Code-based environment with AI assistance. Antigravity was tested by several members and found inconsistent, with reports that it tends to corrupt code on longer running tasks by deleting sections unnecessarily, then unreliably reinserting them from history.

For advanced users, Claude Code (CLI or VS Code extension) offers the most control. The CLI version gets updates first and provides the most flexibility, while the VS Code extension offers better visual feedback. Claude Code's sub-agent capability allows it to spin up helper agents autonomously for research and implementation tasks.

sections.title: Sharing and Distribution
sections.content:
The most discussed challenge is not building tools but distributing them. Open-sourcing on GitHub is the default approach, but as one contributor noted, the main problem is that lawyers do not know how to install it. Most potential users do not have GitHub accounts, VS Code, or the ability to run command-line tools.

Several distribution approaches were discussed. Hosting on Vercel works well for frontend-only applications but raises data security concerns if the app momentarily holds user data on the server instance. Docker containers provide isolation but add another layer of complexity for end users. Packaging as portable executables was suggested but not widely tested. Creating shared mailbox frontends with Power Automate backends was successfully used by one contributor within a corporate environment, bypassing IT restrictions entirely.

The community ultimately launched a platform to showcase deployed tools, with a unified token management system planned so that users receive tokens on sign-in and creators' API costs are covered by the platform rather than individual developers.

sections.title: Data Security Considerations
sections.content:
Data security emerged as a critical concern for lawyer vibe coders. The discussion revealed a spectrum of approaches, from those taking a super strict stance on local-only processing to in-house counsel with higher risk tolerance using enterprise cloud APIs.

Key issues raised include legal privilege concerns when data transits through US-based cloud servers, the difference between consumer and enterprise API terms for data retention and training, the distinction between ZDR (Zero Data Retention) agreements and genuine data isolation, and GDPR compliance for EU-based practitioners.

One contributor pointed out that even with ZDR, data arises momentarily on a US server and was potentially subject to the litigation hold in the NYT v OpenAI case, meaning data that OpenAI was ordered not to delete. This observation challenged several community members' assumptions about the safety of enterprise API agreements.

The practical compromise that emerged was local processing for confidential data and cloud APIs for general legal research, with the possibility of building anonymisation layers that strip identifying information before farming queries out to cloud models.

sections.title: The GSD Framework and Structured Approaches
sections.content:
Several structured approaches to vibe coding were discussed. The GSD (Get Stuff Done) framework was praised by multiple contributors as a game changer that applies rigorous product development methodology to AI-assisted coding. One contributor described it as the difference between drawing with crayon (bare terminal instructions) and drafting blueprints with a team of architects.

GSD establishes roadmaps, development strategy, validation, and ship criteria before directing agents to implement. It was described as really good at context management, maintaining a cohesive roadmap and strategy across many 200k-token sessions. Some contributors run GSD plan phases overnight so code is ready in the morning.

The BMAD method was also highlighted for test-driven development that follows agile methodology, described as essential scaffolding for building anything with proper authentication, security, and API routes.

references: Jordan Bryan - "The Future of Legal Tech Will Be Built By Lawyers" (The Redline)
BMAD Method - for test-driven vibe coding
Derek Schwede - "Start Fresh" skill for Claude Code project reboots
Scott Kveton - "So You Vibe Coded a Legaltech App" series (case.dev)
openQuestions: What is the minimum viable distribution method for lawyer-built tools that preserves data security?
Can vibe-coded legal applications ever reach production quality, or is the ceiling internal tools and prototypes?
How should the community handle the tension between open-sourcing tools and the inability of most lawyers to install them?

tool-showcase.title: Community Tool Showcase
tool-showcase.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 22 contributors

keyFindings.insight: The community has produced an extraordinary breadth of legal AI tools in under 4 months, ranging from document review platforms to gamified legal simulators, demonstrating that lawyer-developers can build sophisticated applications with AI assistance.

The most impactful tools solve specific, concrete workflow bottlenecks rather than attempting general-purpose legal AI. Narrow focus on PE side letters, HKEX size tests, or e-discovery test data produces tools that are immediately useful.

One community member participated in what is believed to be the first Commercial Court-approved deployment of GenAI in a trial, demonstrating that lawyer-built tools can achieve production-grade reliability.

practicalConsensus: Start with a narrow use case you encounter daily rather than trying to build a general-purpose legal AI tool.
Use established tech stacks (React + Vite for frontend, FastAPI or Express for backend) rather than experimenting with exotic frameworks.
Open-source your tools on GitHub even if most users cannot install them -- other developers in the community can learn from and build upon your work.
Include sample data or demo modes so potential users can evaluate the tool without providing their own confidential documents.

activeDebates.question: Should lawyer-built tools aim for production quality or remain prototypes and proofs of concept?

positions.position: Focus on prototypes that demonstrate the concept. Production quality requires security hardening, error handling, and edge case coverage that goes beyond what vibe coding typically achieves.

With proper methodology (GSD, BMAD, test-driven development), lawyer-built tools can absolutely reach production quality. The key is starting with architecture documents and building in backend functionality before features.
The right answer depends on the audience. Internal tools for personal use can stay as prototypes. Anything shared with colleagues or clients needs hardening.

sections.title: Tabular Review
sections.content:
A full-stack AI-powered document review workspace built with React 19, Vite, TypeScript on the frontend and FastAPI + Docling on the backend. The tool transforms unstructured legal contracts into structured, queryable datasets.

Designed for PE side letter analysis, it supports PDF/DOCX upload with backend conversion to markdown, AI extraction of structured data using Gemini models (supporting Gemini 3 Pro, 2.5 Pro, and 2.5 Flash), an Excel-like spreadsheet interface with verification features, citation verification by highlighting exact source quotes in documents, and an AI chat analyst for querying across entire datasets.

The tool includes 8 sample PE side letters with 6 pre-configured extraction columns (Investor Entity, Date, Commitment Amount, MFN clause, Co-Investment Rights, Power of Attorney). A comprehensive build prompt was shared with the community to enable others to recreate the tool.

sections.title: Word Add-In
sections.content:
A self-taught lawyer-developer built a Word add-in similar to Spellbook that integrates AI capabilities directly into Microsoft Word. The project achieved over 1,000 downloads, a significant milestone for a lawyer-built tool.

The developer encountered the Microsoft Partner barrier as a significant obstacle to wider distribution. The add-in demonstrates that individual developers can create tools competitive with commercial offerings, though the distribution challenges through Microsoft's ecosystem remain formidable.

The developer reported that Antigravity (their development environment) tends to corrupt code on longer running tasks, requiring frequent undos and restarts, but the pairing of multiple development tools (Antigravity plus Roo Code) helped mitigate this.

sections.title: Legal Matter Management Platform
sections.content:
An agent-based matter management system focused on legal budgeting and work scoping. The platform models the data structure of legal matters (phases, tasks, budgets) and tests whether significant matter/phase/task structure helps an agent build better budgets than just asking for a budget directly.

The project explores the right granularity of tools for agent interaction: whether to give the agent direct database access with the schema and let it navigate data as it wishes, or constrain it with more structured tools. The developer, who built several big-firm-friendly knowledge portals, noted that speed of inference matters less in legal than in other domains, and that when a model "takes its time," it actually inspires more confidence in the results.

sections.title: ReelDiscovery
sections.content:
A creative tool for generating fake evidence for e-discovery testing purposes. Rather than using real confidential documents for testing disclosure workflows and AI tools, ReelDiscovery generates realistic but synthetic test data that can be freely shared and used for training and evaluation.

This addresses a significant gap in the legal AI space: the difficulty of obtaining representative test data when real legal documents are privileged and confidential.

sections.title: Local RAG System
sections.content:
A fully local RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline built for litigation documents. The system ingests 137,000 Enron emails as test data, uses BAAI/bge-large as the embedding model, and generates responses with Nemotron 3 Nano for inference. Full generation completes in approximately one minute with direct links to underlying source documents for verification.

The system is designed for fact retrieval and summarisation, with the developer noting that analytical tasks do not work as well. The local-only architecture addresses privilege and data security concerns, operating entirely without cloud connectivity. The developer runs it on a GMKTEC Evo X2 mini PC with 128GB unified memory, which they identified as offering the best value for local inference hardware.

sections.title: Humane Calendar
sections.content:
A scheduling and calendar management tool designed to make meeting coordination more humane. The project was shared with the community for beta testing, with discussion about potential Calendly integration to read and pull availability from existing calendaring systems.

The developer acknowledged limited market research but saw potential in improving the meeting scheduling experience for legal professionals who spend significant time coordinating across time zones.

sections.title: The Closing & The Moot Court
sections.content:
A pair of gamified legal simulation tools. The Closing is a gamified SPA (Single Page Application) simulator, while The Moot Court is a multi-agent NDA negotiation simulator. These tools won the London Corporate Harvey Competition, demonstrating that lawyer-built applications can compete with and beat commercial offerings in focused competitions.

The projects demonstrate the potential for AI-powered legal training tools, combining game mechanics with realistic legal scenarios to create engaging learning experiences.

sections.title: Contract Amendment Consolidation
sections.content:
A Gemini-powered tool for parsing contract amendments and tracking clause lifecycles. The tool takes a series of amendments to a contract and consolidates them into a single view showing the current state of each clause, its history of modifications, and the provenance of each change.

This addresses a common pain point in contract management where multiple amendments make it difficult to determine the current operative terms.

sections.title: Mac Docling Shortcut
sections.content: A macOS Shortcut that wraps the Docling document converter, enabling drag-and-drop conversion of PDFs and DOCX files to markdown without any command-line interaction. This lowers the barrier to entry for non-technical lawyers who need markdown conversion for their AI workflows.

sections.title: Corporate Structure Planner
sections.content:
A tool for planning corporate structures and transactions, functioning as a mix between a spreadsheet and Excalidraw diagram. It enables simple modelling of balances and taxes within a year for various corporate structures, primarily designed as a client presentation tool.

The developer noted that while Gemini or Claude artifacts can handle simple structures, more complex multi-entity arrangements cause the artifact to get overcomplicated and break down, necessitating a dedicated local tool.

sections.title: Chronos (Litigation Timeline)
sections.content: A litigation timeline tool for visualising and managing the chronological sequence of events in legal disputes. Timeline construction is a fundamental litigation task and the tool aims to automate the process of extracting dates and events from case documents.

sections.title: RAG Pipeline & Chronology Builder
sections.content: Built by a contributor with an ethical hacking background, this RAG pipeline and chronology builder was involved in one of the first Commercial Court-approved deployments of GenAI in a trial. The developer also built a translation workflow and various internal tools, demonstrating that production-grade legal AI deployment is achievable by individual developers with strong technical foundations.

sections.title: DPA Automation
sections.content:
A data processing agreement automation workflow built using Zapier, Todoist, and Ironclad. The tool was created by an in-house counsel to automate the review and processing of DPAs while keeping a human in the loop. It represents the integration-first approach to legal AI, connecting existing tools rather than building from scratch.

The developer previously used Spellbook but found it insufficient for their workflow, leading them to build a custom solution combining multiple SaaS tools with AI orchestration.

sections.title: Debtors Ledger Tools
sections.content:
Power Automate-based tools for managing delinquent debt recovery workflows. The tools include automatic renaming and saving of monthly aged trial balances, AI summarisation of debtors ledger notes consolidated into reports, and a Copilot agent for users to interact with to add new matters, expenses, and recoveries to SharePoint lists.

The developer works in credit recovery and noted that significant manual data entry across multiple ERPs and data sources makes this a high-value automation target, though data quality issues across different systems remain a challenge.

references: Tabular Review - GitHub (open source, React + Vite + FastAPI)
openQuestions: What is the best distribution mechanism for lawyer-built tools that non-technical legal professionals can actually use?
Should there be a "HuggingFace for legal tech" with vetted and reviewed projects, tools, and a clever mascot?
How can the community monetise tools for creators who do not want to build businesses around their apps?

tool-reviews.title: Opinions & Reviews of Specific Tools and Models
tool-reviews.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 28 contributors

keyFindings.insight: Claude (Anthropic) has emerged as the community's preferred AI provider overall, with Claude Code and Opus 4.5 receiving the strongest endorsements. Multiple contributors cancelled competing subscriptions in favour of Claude Max.
No single model or tool is best for all tasks. The community consensus is to maintain access to multiple providers and switch based on the specific task at hand.

Harvey is respected as a productivity tool for mid-senior lawyers but criticised for technical limitations including GPT-4 default, 50K token context window, and lack of markdown conversion. Multiple community members believe individual developers can build tools that match or exceed Harvey's capabilities in narrow domains.
Local models are rapidly closing the gap with frontier models, now estimated at only 4-5 months behind, with Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen 3.5 receiving particularly strong reviews for legal tasks.

practicalConsensus: Claude Max offers the best value for heavy vibe coders, with unlimited Claude Code usage for a flat monthly fee.
Gemini 3 Pro is worth using for general questions and knowledge retrieval, but Claude remains superior for analytical precision and coding tasks.
For local inference, Ollama is the community standard with 10 votes in the local models poll. Nemotron 3 Nano 30B is highly recommended for legal document tasks.
Cursor Pro is good but limits are frustrating for heavy users. Claude Code CLI provides more control and gets updates first.
Harvey is useful within large firms that have it, but individual developers can replicate much of its functionality for specific use cases.

activeDebates.question: Which AI provider should you consolidate on if you can only afford one subscription?

positions.position: Claude Max. Claude Code plus Opus 4.5 covers 90% of use cases. Cancelled Gemini and Cursor subscriptions.
Cursor Ultra at $200/month gives $400 of inference. Models can work continuously for 45 minutes at a time. The cost-per-capability ratio is hard to beat.
Antigravity with Gemini Pro for the free coding environment, supplemented by Claude Code API calls when needed.

activeDebates.question: Will Google ultimately win the AI race?

positions.position: Google has all the data they need and is likely to win. Their infrastructure advantages are insurmountable.
Google seems confused and unfocused. With Antigravity, Jules, AI Studio, and Gemini CLI, they appear fickle and unable to commit to one direction.
No Google model has ever had that thing -- analytical precision and insight. Maybe for general purpose but not for professionals.

sections.title: AI Models: Claude (Anthropic)
sections.content:
Opus 4.5 received the most positive reviews of any model discussed. It was praised for complicated tasks, planning, and tool use, with one contributor noting it is definitely an improvement over Gemini 3 Pro. For refactoring, it was the unanimous first recommendation. Multiple contributors described it as having analytical precision and insight that other models lack.

However, Opus 4.5 experiences inconsistency. One contributor described it as now stupid on a particular day, while others found it noticeably better that same week. The consensus is that instance-level inference inconsistency exists but the model's ceiling is the highest available.

Claude Max subscription was endorsed by several contributors as the best value. One contributor noted they never hit limits with Claude Code on the subscription, while paying only a fraction of what Cursor Ultra costs. Claude Code CLI was preferred over the VS Code extension for having the most control and getting features first.

sections.title: AI Models: GPT (OpenAI)
sections.content:
GPT-5.x series received mixed reviews. Several contributors maintained ChatGPT subscriptions out of inertia but acknowledged that Claude and Gemini had overtaken it for most tasks. Multiple community members were considering cancelling their OpenAI subscriptions.

GPT-5.2 Codex was noted as useful for code execution tasks at a competitive price. The reasoning models (which no longer support temperature adjustment, breaking existing code) received criticism for removing a key parameter for hallucination control.

Harvey's use of GPT-4 as a default was cited as a significant limitation. The fact that users must know how to switch models to access better options was seen as a design failure.

sections.title: AI Models: Gemini (Google)
sections.content:
Gemini 3 Pro was described as a very good model and an improvement over 2.5 Pro, particularly for general questions and knowledge retrieval. One contributor switched to it as their first-choice model for non-coding tasks. However, it was criticised for annoying tendencies like forcing topic-specific metaphors and generating absurd headings.

For coding, Gemini was found to get users to an MVP but then hit a context wall even though it should have a huge context window. The recommendation was to use Gemini for planning and grunt work but rely on Claude for execution of complex changes.

Gemini 2.5 Flash was noted for speed advantages in high-volume processing. Gemini's vision capabilities for OCR and document understanding were praised as having improved substantially.

sections.title: AI Models: Local Models
sections.content:
Nemotron 3 Nano (NVIDIA) was described as an absolute game changer by a contributor who tested it extensively for legal document tasks. Running on a 128GB unified memory mini PC, it provides longer, quicker, and more accurate responses with visible reasoning, completing tasks in approximately 30 seconds. The hybrid SSM/LLM architecture was highlighted as particularly effective.

Qwen 3.5 models received strong praise, with the 35B parameter variant being the first open-source model to correctly cite Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024), a new Supreme Court decision. One contributor claimed a new 9B model beating GPT-OSS-20B at legal reasoning would represent a complete paradigm shift.

The Ollama poll showed it as the dominant local inference platform (10 votes), followed by LM Studio (4 votes), VSCode + LocalAI (3 votes), coding agents (2 votes), and OpenWebUI, Jan.ai, and Msty with 1 vote each. Llamafile (Mozilla) was also recommended.

sections.title: Coding Tools
sections.content:
Claude Code was the community's preferred coding tool, with the CLI version favoured for maximum control. Sub-agent capability was praised, with the model autonomously spinning up helper agents for research and exploration. The Cowork feature was tested extensively, with results described as having a lot of potential but requiring skill development to get consistent results on complex tasks. A known Windows compatibility issue prevents Cowork from working on some PCs.

Cursor received praise as a solid coding environment but frustration over rate limits on the Pro plan. One contributor noted Cursor Pro limits drove them to commit fully to Claude Code instead. Cursor Ultra at $200/month was valued by those who could justify the expense.

Antigravity (Google) was described as inconsistent and not too reliable. Specific criticisms included code corruption on longer running tasks, where it deletes sections unnecessarily and then unreliably reinserts them. The Gemini Pro backend was noted as its main advantage, providing free inference on a capable model.

Cline was mentioned as a tool for using local models for coding, with one contributor asking if there is anything better. The response pointed to Claude Code with Ollama as an alternative.

sections.title: Document Processing Tools
sections.content:
Docling (IBM) was the most recommended document processing tool, consistently suggested when members asked about document conversion. It was used as the backend for the Tabular Review project and several other community tools.

MarkItDown (Microsoft) was acknowledged for breadth of format support but considered less precise than Docling for complex legal documents specifically.

The Word MCP was tested by multiple contributors and found wanting. It was described as not very intuitive with functionality that is not as good as generating a script on the fly. It fails on writing to DOCX files regularly.

superdoc-redlines was praised as a purpose-built solution for legal document comparison. Its core library is only 196 lines of code, embodying the principle that figuring out what not to build was the hard part.

sections.title: Platforms: Harvey, Legora, Spellbook
sections.content:
Harvey was the most extensively discussed commercial platform. A mid-senior M&A lawyer provided a detailed review, praising it for email drafting, preliminary due diligence, transaction document brainstorming, negotiation pressure-testing, and document comparison. Harvey was credited with genuinely reducing time and effort, with tasks that normally take 10 times as long getting done the same morning.

Criticisms of Harvey included: GPT-4 as the default model with model switching requiring technical knowledge, only 50K token context window per prompt, all uploaded files being vectorised with no simple markdown conversion, and general cross-referencing checks not being great. The absence of workflow automation usage even among regular users was noted.

Legora was described as having a visual workflow builder for chaining actions together. One contributor from a firm using it noted that in quite some aspects it still feels quite simple and not too big a jump from paid subscriptions to frontier models.

Spellbook was mentioned by an in-house counsel who had tried it but wondered if there is a better solution, leading them to build custom DPA automation instead.

sections.title: Infrastructure: Ollama, LM Studio, Hosting
sections.content:
Ollama was the dominant local inference platform, recently adding cloud capabilities (Ollama Cloud) that were praised by several contributors. The platform's simplicity and broad model support make it the default recommendation for anyone getting started with local models.

LM Studio received 4 votes in the community poll as a preferred local inference tool. It was mentioned less frequently than Ollama but valued by those who prefer its GUI-based approach.

For hosting, Hetzner was mentioned by a contributor planning to run a virtual machine for Claude Code access from mobile devices. Vercel was the most popular deployment target for web applications, with Railway also receiving positive mentions. One contributor described Railway as working well, with good results from telling Claude to read the documentation and make the project compatible.

sections.title: Frameworks: GSD, MCP, Skills
sections.content:
The GSD (Get Stuff Done) framework received enthusiastic reviews. One contributor described it as the difference between drawing with crayon and drafting blueprints with a team of architects. It was credited with being really good at context management and maintaining cohesive roadmaps across many 200k-token sessions. However, it was also described as disconcerting because every change requires 5 phases and the coder must hold their breath through hours of work hoping for the right result.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) was discussed in the context of tool integration. One contributor initially disliked MCP as adding another layer of abstraction but came to see two benefits: it helps with tool selection in a tool-heavy environment and allows easier security gateway integration. The consensus was that CLI tools can be easily refactored into MCP when needed.

The Claude Skills system was actively explored, with community members downloading, testing, and creating custom skills. Security skills were available but their effectiveness was questioned. The Claude legal plugin was analysed by the community, who found it shipped 6 skills and 5 commands with an Apache 2.0 license.

sections.title: Microsoft Copilot Ecosystem
sections.content:
Microsoft Copilot received consistently lukewarm reviews. It was described as terrible for workflow creation, a weak version of Claude in Excel, and behind the frontier with deliberately cheap models to save on inference costs. The context window was described as too short for long conversations.

However, the M365 integration was acknowledged as its killer feature. For organisations already in the Microsoft ecosystem, Copilot provides corporate-data-safe AI access that other tools cannot match. The upgrade to GPT-5.x made a noticeable difference, and the ability to create agents within Copilot Chat was valued despite limitations.

Using GitHub Copilot to originate legal advice documents by treating past examples and legislation as code was described as producing significantly better output than the standard Copilot agent builder, as the model reasons over documents more logically in a code context.

The Copilot Studio premium licensing structure was described as confusing, with multiple contributors struggling to understand the different tiers and capabilities.

openQuestions: Will the rapid improvement in local models make cloud-based legal AI products obsolete within a year?
Is there a meaningful quality difference between Claude Code CLI and the VS Code extension for complex legal coding tasks?
Can Microsoft Copilot close the gap with Claude and Gemini within 6 months as predicted by some community members?
Should the community develop standardised evals for comparing inference plans and model performance on legal tasks?

contract-review-ai.title: Contract Review with AI Agents
contract-review-ai.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 8 contributors

keyFindings.insight: A 400-scenario procurement playbook was tested with Claude Cowork for contract review, revealing that attention degradation on long documents causes the AI to miss critical issues like IP indemnity clauses.

Separating analysis of deviations from redlining into distinct phases produces better results than attempting both in a single pass. The attention problem and context window limitations make combined workflows unreliable.

Playbook generation from golden standard templates is achievable but requires significant refinement. Granular scenarios (e.g. whether to accept government regulations as a force majeure event) are too detailed for current AI attention spans in a single pass.

The Cowork refinement process -- iteratively developing and testing skills with Claude -- produces progressively better results, but out of the box you need some work to get Cowork to perform complex tasks.

Windows compatibility issues with Claude Cowork prevent some users from accessing the feature at all, with a permanent "failed to start Claude's workspace" error that appears to be a known but unresolved bug.

practicalConsensus: Shorten and prioritise your playbook for AI use. A 400-scenario playbook is overkill for current AI attention capabilities. Focus on critical clauses.
Separate the analysis phase (identifying deviations from your playbook) from the redlining phase (marking up the document). Run them as distinct steps.
Use the Cowork refinement process to iteratively improve skills rather than expecting perfect results on the first attempt.
Consider running analysis separately for each playbook entry with a redline orchestration layer, rather than feeding the entire playbook and contract in one pass.
Use red/yellow/green classification for quick triage of issues before detailed analysis.
Test on known documents where you already know the correct answers to validate your workflow before deploying on new contracts.

activeDebates.question: Clause-by-clause review vs whole-document review: which approach produces better results?

positions.position: Running analysis separately for each prompt/playbook entry with a redline orchestration layer is more reliable. Each pass can focus its full attention on a specific issue.
Whole-document review with a shortened playbook is more practical. Running hundreds of separate passes is too slow for real-world contract turnaround times.
A hybrid approach works best: first pass for red-flag identification across the whole document, then targeted clause-by-clause analysis for flagged issues.

activeDebates.question: How should playbooks be structured for AI consumption?

positions.position: Start from existing firm playbooks (spreadsheets of scenarios with standard language and alternatives) and convert them into AI skills. The knowledge already exists, it just needs reformatting.
AI-native playbooks should be structured differently from human playbooks. Separate the "what to look for" from the "how to respond" and use explicit priority rankings.
Use golden standard templates as the ground truth. Let the AI compare the contract against the template and flag deviations rather than encoding rules explicitly.

sections.title: The 400-Scenario Playbook Test
sections.content:
A significant real-world experiment was conducted using Claude Cowork with a procurement contract playbook containing over 400 different scenarios. The playbook, developed over three years by a procurement team, covered standard language and 2-3 alternative scenarios for each clause, including granular considerations such as whether government regulations should be accepted as a force majeure event.

Early testing showed the contract review to be very accurate on many provisions. However, a critical failure was identified: the system did not pick up the lack of an IP indemnity clause, described as pretty important for the customer side of a procurement agreement. This missed finding demonstrated that even when AI performs well on the majority of provisions, the failure mode of missing critical issues makes unchecked AI review dangerous.

The conclusion was that the playbook was too granular for AI consumption in its current form. Refinement was needed to shorten it and focus on critical clauses while still capturing the essential scenarios.

sections.title: Attention Degradation on Long Documents
sections.content:
The missed IP indemnity finding was attributed to an attention problem. When the document is too long and the playbook is too detailed, the AI simply misses things. This is consistent with known limitations of transformer-based models where attention degrades over long contexts, even when the nominal context window is large enough to hold all the content.

One contributor suspected this is an inherent architectural issue: the model reads the long playbook, reads the contract, and then attempts to assess against all criteria simultaneously. By the time it reaches certain provisions, earlier playbook entries may have effectively faded from active attention.

This finding reinforced the argument for breaking the analysis into multiple focused passes rather than attempting comprehensive review in a single shot.

sections.title: Separate Analysis from Redlining
sections.content:
The community converged on the architectural principle that analysis (identifying what needs to change) and redlining (making the changes in the document) should be separate phases. Running them together creates two compounding problems: the attention problem when reading both playbook and contract, plus the DOCX manipulation challenge when trying to make precise edits.

The proposed architecture involves a first phase of deviation analysis where the contract is assessed clause-by-clause against the playbook, producing a structured list of findings with classification (red/yellow/green) and suggested language. This is followed by a second phase of redline orchestration where each identified deviation is addressed individually, making targeted edits to the DOCX file.

This separation allows using different tools for each phase: markdown-based analysis for the first phase (where accuracy is paramount) and DOCX manipulation tools like superdoc-redlines for the second phase (where formatting fidelity matters).

sections.title: Cowork Refinement Process
sections.content:
Several contributors described iterative refinement of Cowork skills as essential for achieving good results. The process involves creating an initial skill based on the playbook, testing it against known contracts, identifying failures, refining the skill's instructions, and repeating.

One contributor recommended engaging with Claude Cowork to develop the refinements, noting that if you have a template or description of your workflows, start from that and refine it with the tool. Creating new skills using the Skill Builders within Claude was described as progressively more effective.

The use of Cowork's built-in refinement capabilities rather than manual skill editing was found to produce better results, as the AI can identify gaps in its own instructions that humans might miss.

sections.title: Playbook Generation from Templates
sections.content:
The question of generating AI-consumable playbooks from golden standard templates was raised. The approach involves taking a firm's standard template contract with footnotes and comments that capture the negotiation guidance, then using AI to deduce the review rules from these annotations.

This approach was described as feasible but requiring careful prompt engineering. The footnotes and "eyebrows" (header/footer annotations) in templates carry critical context about when to accept or reject certain language, what the firm's bottom-line position is, and what alternative formulations are acceptable.

The challenge is that template annotations were written for human lawyers and assume significant background knowledge. Converting these into explicit, machine-readable rules requires understanding the implicit context and making it explicit.

sections.title: Windows Compatibility Issues
sections.content:
Multiple contributors reported that Claude Cowork does not function on Windows machines, with a permanent "failed to start Claude's workspace / failed to reach Claude API" error. This was described as a known error that blocks access to the entire Cowork feature.

The issue appears inconsistent: one contributor confirmed it works on their PC while others reported complete failure. The Chat and Code features work fine on the same machines, suggesting the issue is specific to the Cowork workspace initialisation.

For teams considering deploying contract review workflows based on Cowork skills, this compatibility limitation is a significant concern, as many law firm environments run Windows exclusively.

references: Claude Cowork - Anthropic's collaborative AI workspace
Claude Skills system - for encoding workflow knowledge
Anthropic Claude Code security research preview
openQuestions: What is the optimal playbook length and granularity for reliable AI contract review with current models?
Can a redline orchestration layer be built that reliably combines per-clause analysis into a coherent set of DOCX edits?
When will the Windows Cowork compatibility issue be resolved, and is there a workaround?
How should contract review accuracy be benchmarked -- what constitutes an acceptable miss rate for AI-assisted review?

claude-code.title: Claude Code for Legal Professionals
claude-code.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 28 contributors spanning legal, in-house, and legal-tech roles

keyFindings.insight: The CLI version of Claude Code consistently receives more features and updates first, but running it inside the VS Code terminal provides the best of both worlds: full CLI power with file/folder visibility in the IDE sidebar.

The GSD (Get Shit Done) framework transforms vibe coding into structured product development. It applies rigorous PM-style roadmaps, validation, and ship criteria across multiple 200k-token sessions, maintaining cohesive context throughout.

Claude Code Skills (custom .md instruction files triggered contextually) are a significant upgrade over the CustomGPT paradigm. They are auto-triggered, stackable, support progressive disclosure, and can be updated in-context without restarting sessions.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) adds value in two key ways for legal practitioners: it helps with tool selection in tool-heavy environments, and it allows plugging in a security gateway more easily. CLI tools can be easily refactored into MCP servers.

An arxiv study (2602.11988) found that CLAUDE.md and agent.md instruction files may actually result in worse coding outcomes than including no instruction files at all, calling into question the common practice of elaborate prompt scaffolding.

Mobile access to Claude Code evolved from early Tailscale/SSH setups through third-party apps like Happy Code, to the official Claude iOS app gaining native Claude Code support around late February 2026.
Agent Teams (new for Opus 4.6) enables significantly more work in a single shot compared to single-agent-with-subagents, and must be specifically enabled in the config file.

Budget management is a real concern. Community spending ranges from $50/month at the conservative end to $200+ at the heavy-usage end, with the Max plan ($100/month for extended Opus access) widely regarded as excellent value.

positions.position: In-house teams at tech companies can often access Claude Code through Amazon Bedrock with full organisational approval. Some GCs are explicitly AI-forward with no restrictions.
Large institutional investors (AU$200B+ fund example) remain cautious with a "cannot trust it" mindset described as "still so 2024".
IT gatekeeping and backend access restrictions make Claude Code adoption difficult in large firms, despite strong individual interest.
Enterprise access routes often go through Azure endpoints. GDPR and profiling restrictions add complexity to deployment.

practicalConsensus: Run Claude Code in the terminal inside VS Code for the best balance of CLI features and visual file navigation.
Use the GSD framework for greenfield projects and major refactoring work. It structures development into five phases per change with validation gates.
Set up budget limits before starting. The Max plan at $100/month provides strong Opus access, but API-key-based usage can escalate quickly without caps.
Install the Superpowers plugin for systemic debugging. Some of its features are being integrated into Plan mode natively by Anthropic.
For overnight or long-running tasks, use GSD plan-phase with auto mode so code is ready on wake-up. Slower inference can paradoxically increase confidence in results.
Always set up .gitignore and deny .env access before starting any project. Agents will attempt to read secrets despite explicit restrictions.
Try different models when stuck. Gemini 3.1 through GitHub Copilot has resolved issues where Opus 4.6 gets caught in loops on specific problems.
Convert the Max subscription into API access through proxy methods for heavier automated workflows, but be aware of ToS risks.

activeDebates.question: Do CLAUDE.md instruction files actually improve or degrade coding outcomes?

positions.position: An arxiv study shows instruction files result in worse outcomes. Many instructions are redundant with what the model already knows, adding noise rather than signal.
Instruction files are essential for maintaining project context, coding conventions, and avoiding repeated mistakes across sessions. The study may not reflect real-world usage patterns.
The answer is likely nuanced. Minimal, high-signal instructions help. Verbose or redundant instructions hurt. The key is keeping them lean.

activeDebates.question: Is spec-driven development worth the overhead for legal projects?

positions.position: Spec-driven development with tools like GitHub Speckit provides structure. Worth exploring for larger projects.
GSD already provides adequate structure. Adding another spec layer on top may be over-engineering for most legal-tech builds.

activeDebates.question: VS Code extension vs pure CLI vs terminal-in-IDE: which setup is optimal?

positions.position: Pure terminal gives most control and gets features first. VS Code terminal within the IDE is the sweet spot.
The VS Code extension with Cowork mode is better for certain "OS" type tasks and provides a more visual workflow.
Running Claude Code inside Cursor (without a Cursor subscription) works well because of UI refinements over VS Code and avoids Microsoft update noise.

sections.title: Getting Started: CLI vs Extension vs Terminal-in-IDE
sections.content:
The community overwhelmingly favours running Claude Code in the terminal, but the question of which terminal context matters. The official Claude documentation confirms that the CLI version exposes more commands and features (including bash shortcuts, tab completion, and slash commands) than the VS Code extension. However, running the CLI inside VS Code's integrated terminal provides the best practical workflow: you get full CLI power while being able to see file changes, folder structure, and open files in the IDE sidebar.

Several contributors reported that VS Code's terminal passes additional context from open editor windows to Claude Code, which can be both helpful (it sees what you are working on) and occasionally problematic (it makes unnecessary references to open files). One practitioner runs Claude Code exclusively in a terminal within Cursor, having cancelled their Cursor subscription but retained the IDE for its UI refinements.

For those seeking deeper control, the combination of PowerShell or bash terminals with WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) for separate workloads like Codex provides a clean separation of concerns. Terminal multiplexers like Tmux are used by some for managing multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions.

sections.title: The GSD Framework: Structured Vibe Coding
sections.content:
The GSD (Get Shit Done) framework emerged as one of the most discussed tools in the community. Contributors describe it as transformative for both greenfield and brownfield projects. The framework applies product development methodology to vibe coding, functioning like having a PM from a major tech company establishing roadmaps, development strategy, validation criteria, and ship criteria before directing agents to execute.

Each change requires five phases of work, which initially feels disconcerting as contributors describe holding their breath through hours of coding before seeing results. However, the structured approach produces significantly more coherent and production-ready output than bare instructions in the terminal, which one contributor characterised as "drawing with crayon" compared to GSD's "drafting blueprints with a team of architects."

GSD is particularly strong at context management, maintaining a cohesive roadmap and strategy across many 200k-token sessions. For overnight work, contributors recommend running the plan-phase with auto mode so the code is ready on wake-up. This approach also prevents the habit of staring at logs, which multiple contributors acknowledged as a problematic tendency.

A recent fork of GSD integrates RLM (Recursive Language Models) to allow running through entire projects while keeping the main orchestrator within context bounds.

sections.title: Mobile Access: From Hacks to Official Support
sections.content:
Mobile access to Claude Code has been a recurring request. Early solutions included using Tailscale VPN on a phone combined with an SSH client (Terminus) to connect to a private server hosting a Claude Code instance. Another workaround involved Chrome Remote Desktop to a home machine, though this was described as deeply clunky and nearly impossible for GSD's interactive discussion flow.

A third-party app called Happy Code provided a more streamlined mobile experience. The Claude iOS app also gained native Claude Code support, though initially limited compared to the full CLI. The community noted sightings of Claude Code connected to Kindles for outdoor coding and even Apple Watches, though these were treated more as curiosities than practical setups.

By late February 2026, the official Claude mobile app had improved its Claude Code integration significantly, reducing the need for SSH-based workarounds for basic monitoring and prompting tasks.

sections.title: Skills, MCP, and the Plugin Ecosystem
sections.content:
Claude Code Skills represent a significant paradigm shift from the CustomGPT model. Skills are markdown instruction files that are auto-triggered based on context, stackable (multiple skills can be active simultaneously), support progressive disclosure (revealing complexity only when needed), and can be updated in-context without restarting sessions. Contributors access skills from community repositories like skills.sh and through Claude's built-in Skill Builders.

The relationship between Skills and MCP (Model Context Protocol) generated substantial discussion. MCP was initially viewed sceptically as an unnecessary abstraction layer, but the community identified two clear benefits: it helps with tool selection in environments with many tools, and it enables plugging in a security gateway more easily. CLI tools can be refactored into MCP servers relatively straightforwardly.

Practical MCP experiences were mixed. The Word MCP was found to be unintuitive with limited functionality compared to generating scripts on the fly. However, Playwright MCP and agent-browser (a Playwright wrapper) were recommended for build testing. One contributor noted that using an open-source Playwright CLI directly (not via MCP) could be more token-efficient for web app testing.

sections.title: Budget Management and Plan Selection
sections.content:
Cost management is a persistent concern across the community. Monthly spending ranges from under $50 at the careful end to well over $200 for heavy users. The $100/month Max plan is widely considered the best value proposition, providing extended Opus access that would cost $500+ in equivalent API calls.

Some contributors use Ollama Cloud inference at a flat $100/month as an alternative, finding that API calls that would cost $500/month on Anthropic fall well under the subscription price. Others convert their Max subscription into API access through proxy methods, though this carries ToS risks.

When Opus rate limits are hit, contributors switch to alternative models temporarily. One approach involves using Gemini 3.0 during Opus cooldowns, described as "slumming it" but functionally adequate. The anxiety around Opus 4.6 rate limits is real, with contributors questioning whether the improvement over Sonnet 4.5 justifies the faster budget consumption for mundane tasks.

A notable minority takes a more aggressive approach, setting hard budget caps on API keys and not worrying about exposure, reasoning that the budget limits themselves provide sufficient protection.

sections.title: Thinking Visibility and Debugging
sections.content:
A recurring frustration is the inability to follow all of Claude Code's thinking processes. The agent sometimes provides catch-up summaries but otherwise assumes the user does not want to see intermediate reasoning. In the terminal, Ctrl+O reveals thinking, but this is not available in all interfaces.

The Superpowers plugin addresses some of these gaps, particularly through its systemic debugging features. Anthropic has been integrating some Superpowers capabilities directly into Plan mode, including brainstorming features. However, the debugging tools remain distinctly useful.

Contributors noted a paradox around inference speed: slower processing can actually increase confidence in results. When the model takes its time, users feel more assured than when it jumps to an answer quickly. This has practical implications for legal work where accuracy matters more than speed, and where background or overnight batch processing is often acceptable.

references: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.11988 — Study on instruction file effects on coding outcomes
openQuestions: How should dependency management work when Claude Code updates packages without explicit approval?
What is the optimal CLAUDE.md length and content given the arxiv study showing potential degradation?
Can spec-driven development with tools like Speckit meaningfully improve outcomes over GSD alone?
How do Agent Teams scale for large legal-tech projects with multiple concurrent workstreams?

local-models.title: Running Legal AI Locally: Hardware & Models
local-models.synthesisStatement: Synthesised from community discussions across 22 contributors with hardware benchmarking and model evaluation data

keyFindings.insight: Ollama dominates the local model runtime landscape among legal practitioners, receiving 10 out of 22 poll votes. LM Studio (4 votes) and VSCode + LocalAI (3 votes) are the next most popular options.

The AMD Strix Halo with 128GB unified memory at under GBP 2,000 is the recommended hardware for serious local inference, outperforming Mac options at comparable or lower cost for single-user workloads.

NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Nano, a hybrid SSM/LLM architecture, was described as a game changer for local RAG, producing longer, faster, and more accurate responses with visible reasoning in approximately 30 seconds.
Qwen 3.5 became the first open-source model to correctly cite Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (the case that overruled Chevron deference), a benchmark no previous open-source model had achieved.
Local 30-40B parameter models with 60k+ tokens of context can deliver accurate responses in under one minute on optimised Linux setups with proper GPU offloading.

The open-source gap behind frontier models is closing fast, with local models now approximately 4-5 months behind frontier performance and expected to reach current frontier standards within six months.
Speculative decoding provides measurable speed improvements for local RAG pipelines, primarily through faster token generation without quality degradation.

jurisdictionComparisons.aspect: Government stance on local AI models

positions.position: The US government has moved toward local model deployment, creating tensions for contractors who use cloud-based AI tools like Claude for product development. Interpretation of policy remains unclear.
Data residency requirements under GDPR make local models attractive for processing confidential client data without cross-border data transfer concerns.
Contributors report strong interest in local models for international trade and customs work where data sovereignty is paramount.

practicalConsensus: Start with Ollama as the runtime. It has the largest community, best documentation, and integrates directly with tools like Cline and Claude Code.
For hardware, the Strix Halo 128GB offers the best price-to-capability ratio. Ensure GPU offloading is properly configured to avoid CPU thermal issues.
Mac M4 Ultra with 64GB RAM hits a ceiling at 24-30B parameter models. The 128GB Strix Halo significantly extends this range.
Let your IDE configure and optimise the local model setup rather than manual configuration. Build a custom chat interface on top.
Dual-boot Linux/Windows for inference workloads. Linux provides measurable speed improvements over Windows for the same hardware.
Llamafile from Mozilla AI offers a convenient single-binary distribution for local model deployment.
Claude Code can be connected to local models through Ollama with configuration adjustments, providing the Claude Code workflow with local inference.

activeDebates.question: Is fine-tuning still relevant when frontier models are so capable?

positions.position: Traditional fine-tuning and LoRA remain more predictable and useful than novel approaches like Document-to-LoRA. Frontier models with good prompting cover most use cases.
The open-source gap is closing fast. Local models are 4-5 months behind and will reach frontier standards within half a year, making fine-tuning on local models increasingly viable.

Neither LoRA nor new approaches like Document-to-LoRA give you provenance. Information baked into weights cannot be traced back to specific clauses or pages. RAG with source retrieval is still the best approach.

activeDebates.question: Is investing in local hardware economically rational given cloud subsidies?

positions.position: Big tech is subsidising LLMs so heavily that running locally is burning money. A 5090 GPU purchase is hard to justify economically when subscriptions are so cheap.
Local hardware pays for itself in privacy compliance and data sovereignty. For privileged legal work, the cost is justified by avoiding cloud exposure entirely.
The Strix Halo at under GBP 2,000 provides capabilities that would cost many times more in API calls over its lifetime. The breakeven is fast for heavy users.

activeDebates.question: Can the Tinybox justify its $12,000 price for legal professionals?

positions.position: At $12k it is overkill for consumer inference workloads. Not rack-grade but too expensive for individual use.
The Tinybox occupies an awkward middle ground. For serious training you need more, and for inference the Strix Halo does the job at a fraction of the cost.

sections.title: Hardware Landscape for Legal AI
sections.content:
The hardware discussion centres on three main options: Apple Silicon Macs, AMD Strix Halo systems, and dedicated GPU setups like the Tinybox.

The Mac M4 Ultra with 64GB RAM is popular but hits a practical ceiling at 24-30B parameter models. The unified memory architecture (where GPU and CPU share the same memory pool) is an advantage, but 64GB is insufficient for the larger models that produce the best legal reasoning output. A contributor who bought the Mac for roughly $6,000 expressed temptation to get more RAM.

The AMD Strix Halo with 128GB unified memory at under GBP 2,000 emerged as the strong recommendation. Like the Mac, it uses unified memory, so the full 128GB is available for model loading. The key configuration insight is ensuring inference is properly pushed to GPU rather than falling back to CPU, which causes overheating. On Linux, one contributor achieves accurate responses in under one minute with 30-40B parameter models and 60k+ tokens of context.

The Tinybox at $12,000 was discussed but dismissed as overkill for individual inference workloads while insufficient for serious training. Its 64GB of GPU memory is actually less than the Strix Halo's 128GB unified pool.

For all setups, the consensus advice is to let the IDE handle model configuration and optimisation rather than manual tuning, then build a custom chat interface on top.

sections.title: Software Ecosystem: Ollama and Alternatives
sections.content:
A community poll revealed clear preferences: Ollama (10 votes), LM Studio (4 votes), VSCode + LocalAI (3 votes), coding agents like OpenCode and Mistral Code (2 votes), and single votes each for OpenWebUI, Jan.ai, and Msty.

Ollama's dominance stems from its simplicity, broad model support, and integration with other tools. Ollama Cloud offers flat-rate inference at $100/month, which one contributor found covers API calls that would cost $500/month through Anthropic directly. Claude Code can connect to local models through Ollama with some configuration.

LM Studio is favoured for its visual interface that shows which models work on specific hardware. Llamafile from Mozilla AI was recommended for single-binary deployment convenience.

Cline is the primary coding agent for local model usage, though contributors explored using Jan.ai to connect to Claude Code. OpenCode was mentioned as another option. The key workflow is using local models for routine tasks and switching to frontier cloud models for complex reasoning.

sections.title: Model Highlights: Nemotron, Qwen, and Kimi
sections.content:
NVIDIA's Nemotron 3 Nano attracted the most excitement. As a hybrid SSM (State Space Model) and LLM architecture, it produces longer, faster, and more accurate responses than predecessors, with visible reasoning. It was described as an absolute game changer for local RAG workloads, completing queries in approximately 30 seconds.

Qwen 3.5 made a significant breakthrough by becoming the first open-source model to correctly cite Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, the 2024 Supreme Court decision that overruled Chevron deference. Small Qwen 3.5 models showed surprisingly strong performance against much larger models, with the 9B model potentially beating GPT-oss-20B at legal reasoning, though benchmark validity was questioned.

Kimi K2.5 entered the discussion with claims of outperforming GPT 5.2 on coding benchmarks. Contributors found its visual capabilities particularly impressive and began using it for day-to-day tasks through kimi.com and API services like Novita AI.

AgentCPM models from OpenBMB attracted attention for achieving agentic capabilities at remarkably small sizes (4-8B parameters), suggesting the potential for deep research capacity in local models.

sections.title: Deployment Patterns and Claude Code Integration
sections.content:
The community explored several deployment patterns for local models. Full-stack web serving from a local machine is feasible for individual or small-team use, with the Strix Halo or similar hardware handling both inference and serving.

Connecting Claude Code to local models through Ollama was confirmed working, with the ollama.com blog providing setup instructions. This gives practitioners the Claude Code workflow (slash commands, skills, GSD framework) while running inference locally on their own hardware.

Speculative decoding was tested and confirmed to provide speed improvements for local RAG workloads. The technique uses a smaller "draft" model to predict tokens that a larger model then validates, reducing the effective compute per token.

Document-to-LoRA techniques (automatically converting documents into fine-tuning data) were discussed but remain impractical: they require days of pre-training on expensive H200 hardware and lose provenance, making them unsuitable for legal work where traceability is essential.

sections.title: US Government Local Model Policy and DOGE Implications
sections.content:
The US government's move toward local model deployment created significant discussion, particularly around implications for government contractors. The policy raised questions about whether companies providing services to the government would need to stop using cloud-based AI tools like Claude for product development.

Contributors noted this would be counterproductive, effectively taking away tools that help deliver better and more secure products. The challenge of interpreting these policies in practice remains unresolved, with the actual scope of restrictions unclear.

More broadly, the US government's interest in local models validates the approach the community has been exploring. The political weather was described as favourable for Meta and other open-weight model providers, given the potential for massive government procurement of local inference infrastructure.

unsloth.ai — Model quantisation and GGUF tooling

Can Document-to-LoRA techniques ever provide sufficient provenance for legal applications?
How will US government local model policies affect legal-tech companies that use cloud AI?
